name: Black Pope Theory
description: The 'Black Pope Theory' posits that the Superior General of the Society of Jesus (Jesuits) wields more power and influence within the Catholic Church than the Pope himself, who is often referred to as the 'White Pope.' Proponents claim the Jesuit Superior General, due to the order's historical involvement in global politics, finance, and education, secretly controls the Vatican and the wider world. The theory suggests that the Superior General, representing a hidden agenda, manipulates the Pope and Church policies to achieve specific, often malevolent, goals. This theory frequently ties into broader conspiracy narratives involving the New World Order and secret societies. The origins of the theory are difficult to pinpoint but gained traction in the late 20th and early 21st centuries through various online forums, books, and videos promoting conspiracy theories. Evidence cited often includes misinterpreted historical events, selective quotes from Jesuit texts, and the secretive nature attributed to the Society of Jesus. Mainstream historians and Catholic scholars overwhelmingly reject the 'Black Pope Theory,' arguing that it is based on misinformation, historical inaccuracies, and anti-Jesuit sentiment. They emphasize the distinct roles and responsibilities of the Pope and the Superior General, highlighting the Pope's supreme authority within the Church. Critiques also point out the lack of credible evidence supporting the claims of secret control and manipulation.
subtitle: Jesuit Superior General's Alleged Secret Power

claims.claim: The Jesuit Superior General controls the Pope.
claims.evidence: Jesuits' historical influence in politics and education; perceived secrecy of the order.
claims.criticism: The Pope is the supreme authority in the Catholic Church, and the Superior General's role is to lead the Jesuit order, not to control the papacy. No credible evidence supports the claim of control.

claims.claim: The Jesuits have a hidden agenda to control the world.
claims.evidence: Selective interpretations of historical events and Jesuit writings; accusations of involvement in conspiracies.
claims.criticism: This is a broad and unsubstantiated claim lacking concrete evidence. It relies on misinterpretations and historical distortions, often fueled by anti-Jesuit prejudice.

claims.claim: The Black Pope is more powerful than the White Pope.
claims.evidence: Alleged secret oaths and power structures within the Jesuit order.
claims.criticism: The term 'Black Pope' is a colloquialism, not an official title. It does not denote any official power or authority within the Catholic Church. The Pope's authority is absolute and recognized globally by Catholics.

keyFigures.name: Alberto Rivera
keyFigures.role: Former Jesuit priest who promoted anti-Jesuit conspiracy theories.

keyFigures.name: Robert A. Graham
keyFigures.role: Jesuit historian who debunked anti-Jesuit claims and conspiracy theories.

mythologyConnections: The idea of a hidden power behind the throne
Parallel with shadow governments in folklore

timeline.event: The Society of Jesus is founded by Ignatius of Loyola.
The Society of Jesus is suppressed by Pope Clement XIV due to political pressures.
The Society of Jesus is restored by Pope Pius VII.
The 'Black Pope Theory' gains traction online and in conspiracy theory circles.
